raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.7,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":0.7,"R1.4":0.0,"R1.5":0.7,"R1.6":1.0,"R1.7":0.3,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.1","R1.2","R1.3","R1.5","R1.6","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"category_focus":"R1","clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should the improvement focus on surface-level writing polish only, or on substantive research-draft revision including argument, structure, and claim support?","rationale":{"R1.1":"Improving the draft may require reframing the contribution, gap, and thesis beyond sentence-level edits.","R1.2":"A substantive improvement pass would be expected to check whether the argument about verbal-confidence elicitation follows coherently from the reported premises.","R1.3":"The draft makes quantitative claims from tables and benchmarks, so improvement centrally involves ensuring those claims match the reported evidence.","R1.5":"The excerpt mixes methods, results, related work, and ablation discussion, making section-level narrative order a central responsibility.","R1.6":"The open-ended request to improve the draft makes sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, flow, and concision load-bearing.","RX.1":"Any projection must disclose uncertainty because the delegation is broad and could mean polish-only or substantive revision.","RX.2":"The projected responsibilities must avoid treating the reported calibration results as stronger than the artifact supports.","RX.3":"The agent must remain within draft-improvement responsibilities and not execute edits, experiments, or external literature checks.","RX.4":"The projection should avoid enabling misleading scientific claims or unsupported research conclusions.","RX.5":"The projection should preserve traceability to the delegation and artifact features that motivated each active responsibility."}}

parsed.clarification_question: Should the improvement focus on surface-level writing polish only, or on substantive research-draft revision including argument, structure, and claim support?

rationale.R1.1: Improving the draft may require reframing the contribution, gap, and thesis beyond sentence-level edits.
rationale.R1.2: A substantive improvement pass would be expected to check whether the argument about verbal-confidence elicitation follows coherently from the reported premises.
rationale.R1.3: The draft makes quantitative claims from tables and benchmarks, so improvement centrally involves ensuring those claims match the reported evidence.
rationale.R1.5: The excerpt mixes methods, results, related work, and ablation discussion, making section-level narrative order a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.6: The open-ended request to improve the draft makes sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, flow, and concision load-bearing.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection must disclose uncertainty because the delegation is broad and could mean polish-only or substantive revision.
rationale.RX.2: The projected responsibilities must avoid treating the reported calibration results as stronger than the artifact supports.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must remain within draft-improvement responsibilities and not execute edits, experiments, or external literature checks.
rationale.RX.4: The projection should avoid enabling misleading scientific claims or unsupported research conclusions.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should preserve traceability to the delegation and artifact features that motivated each active responsibility.
